Meristics consistent with M. berylina, which is a very common non-native silverside in Sacramento-San Joquin system. Prominent silver/dark stripe and scale cross-hatching is prominent, which generally distinguishes from other native more estuarine/marine topsmelt/jacksmelts.

LabComments: O. mykiss (steelhead/rainbow) and O. tshawytscha (Chinook) most common salmonids in Sacramento system.  Parr marks approx equal to space btn parr marks, 13-14 dorsal fin rays, and 19 anal fin rays fits chinook better than steelhead. Also, the small size of these specimens is not consisistent with steelhead that would be expected to be larger outmigrating smolts if collected at Yolo Bypass. More likely outmigrating Chinook fry.  Note: both specimens have what appear to be clean straight clips of caudal fin, which would be odd for marking fish of this size. Maybe facility damage or clipped for genetic testing when collected at facility?

LabComments: Only 2 liverbearers in Sacramento/San Joaquin system, G. affinis  and Lucania parva. Dorsally flattened head and anal fin insertion anterior of dorsal fin are diagnostic of G. affinis…a distinct taxon.

No caudal spot at length excludes blackfish and hitch and roach (as do other characters).
